topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the kitchen counter, an unwelcome intrusion. I stared at the arrangement—vibrant, almost offensively cheerful roses in shades of crimson and gold. There was no note, just the heavy, silent presentation of petals. My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath my skin. I picked up a stem, the silk cool against my fingertips, and deliberately snapped it. The sound echoed in the quiet apartment, sharp and unforgiving. This was some kind of deliberate provocation. I slammed the box down onto the granite, the resulting thud rattling the cutlery drawer. I couldn't believe the nerve of it.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the wrapped bouquet, my knuckles went white where I gripped the cardboard. The scent of lilies, cloying and saccharine, filled the air, making my chest feel tight. I didn't look at the flowers; my gaze was fixed on the empty space where a card should have been. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped my lips, ragged and shallow. I stood up, pacing the length of the living room, my movements jerky and sharp. Every step felt weighted with resentment. I wanted to snatch the entire thing and hurl it out the window, but I held myself still, forcing my hands to remain unnervingly steady.

[story 3]

I found the flowers sitting on the entryway table, a silent accusation against the dreary afternoon. They were too perfect, too bright, utterly out of place in this suffocating space. A low growl rumbled in my throat, a sound I immediately regretted. I walked over, my boots clicking loudly on the hardwood floor. I didn't touch them immediately. Instead, I circled the arrangement twice, my shoulders hunched forward as if bracing for impact. My eyes narrowed, scanning the room as if searching for the source of this baffling gesture, this unnecessary display. I finally reached out, not to admire, but to grip the vase so tightly my fingers ached.

A low growl rumbled in my throat, a sound I immediately regretted. I walked over, my boots clicking loudly on the hardwood floor. I didn't touch them immediately. My eyes narrowed, scanning the room as if searching for the source of this baffling gesture, this unnecessary display. I finally reached out, not to admire, but to grip the vase so tightly my fingers ached.
